question.text: Any non-compete fee paid by the acquirer to the sellers in a takeover transaction is treated as:

answer.text: A separate payment not subject to SAST Regulations
A goodwill payment disclosed separately in the letter of offer
Part of the offer price and must be factored into the minimum offer price computation
An optional disclosure item at the acquirer's discretion

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under SAST Regulations, any non-compete fee paid by the acquirer to the sellers is considered part of the offer price. This must be factored into the minimum offer price computation to prevent acquirers from circumventing minimum price rules by routing part of the consideration as non-compete payments. **The Trap:** "Separate payment not subject to SAST" is a common misconception — SEBI specifically plugged this loophole to ensure all forms of consideration are captured in the offer price. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series IX (Merchant Banking) tests the comprehensive definition of offer price. Transfer pricing concepts in Series XV (Research Analyst) also touch on hidden consideration. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When reviewing a deal structure, look for any side arrangements like non-compete fees, consulting contracts, or property sales — all may need to be added to the offer price.
